enrichment.short_description: A global online learning marketplace offering courses, professional certificates, and degrees from top universities and companies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: edX was founded in 2012 by Harvard University and MIT as an online course marketplace, and today offers courses, professional certificates, and full degree programs from universities and companies around the world.

Learners can audit most course content for free or pay for a verified track that includes graded work and a certificate. edX also offers stacked credential programs such as MicroMasters, MicroBachelors, and XSeries, as well as full bachelor's and master's degree programs delivered with partner universities.

The site itself is a hosted, commercial platform rather than self-hosted software. Organizations that want to run their own branded learning site instead use the separate open-source Open edX platform, which powers edX.org and hundreds of other independent learning sites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: edX serves individual learners looking to build skills for free, professionals pursuing certificates and career-focused credentials, and prospective students seeking accredited online degrees from partner institutions.

features.title: Audit and Verified tracks
features.description: Most courses can be audited for free, with a paid verified track that adds graded assignments and a certificate.

features.title: Professional certificate programs
features.description: MicroMasters, MicroBachelors, and XSeries programs bundle multiple courses into a stackable credential.

features.title: Full online degree programs
features.description: Bachelor's and master's degrees are offered through edX's university partners.

features.title: Executive education
features.description: Short-form professional programs aimed at working professionals and leadership development.

features.title: Broad catalog
features.description: Courses span AI, data science, programming, business, and many other subjects from recognized institutions.

features.title: Powered by Open edX
features.description: The underlying platform is the open-source Open edX project, which other organizations can self-host independently.

pros: Free auditing gives access to most course content without payment
Courses and degrees come from well-known universities and companies
Stackable credentials (MicroMasters, MicroBachelors) can count toward full degrees
Offers both self-paced and instructor-paced course formats
cons: Verified certificates, credential programs, and degrees require payment
edx.org itself is not self-hosted; running your own instance requires the separate Open edX project
Course quality, pacing, and support vary by partner institution

faqs.question: Is edX free?
faqs.answer: Auditing most courses is free; earning a verified certificate, MicroMasters credential, or degree requires payment.

faqs.question: Can I self-host edX?
faqs.answer: edx.org itself is a hosted service. Organizations that want to run their own instance use the free, open-source Open edX platform separately.

faqs.question: Who created edX?
faqs.answer: edX was founded in 2012 by Harvard University and MIT.

faqs.question: Does edX offer full degrees?
faqs.answer: Yes, edX offers bachelor's and master's degree programs from partner universities alongside individual courses and certificates.

use_cases.title: Free skill building
use_cases.description: Audit courses at no cost to learn a new subject or skill.

use_cases.title: Career credentials
use_cases.description: Earn verified certificates and MicroMasters credentials to advance a career.

use_cases.title: Accredited degrees
use_cases.description: Enroll in accredited online bachelor's or master's degree programs.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: edX offers online courses, professional certificates, and degrees from top universities and companies, with free auditing and paid verified credentials.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source school management software that schools can self-host to manage students, timetables, and communication.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Gibbon was founded in 2010 by Ross Parker at International College Hong Kong and has grown into a free, open-source school platform maintained by the Gibbon Foundation with community contributors on GitHub.

The software is designed as a unified access point for students, teachers, parents, and administrators, covering student information, timetabling, planning, teaching, and assessment. Because it is self-hosted, schools can host it on their own servers, back it up, redistribute it, and modify it as needed.
Gibbon is available in 26 actively translated languages, including right-to-left languages, and its module system is designed to be flexible, extensible, and themeable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Gibbon is used by international schools, public schools, homeschools, and under-resourced or refugee schools around the world that need a free, self-hosted alternative to commercial school management systems.

features.title: Student information management
features.description: Central records for students across enrollment, contacts, and academic history.

features.title: Timetabling tools
features.description: Tools for building and managing class schedules.

features.title: Planning, teaching, and assessment
features.description: Modules that support lesson planning, gradebooks, and assessment tracking.

features.title: Role-based access
features.description: Configurable access levels for students, teachers, parents, and administrators.

features.title: 26-language translation support
features.description: Actively translated interface, including right-to-left text and UTF-8 encoding.

features.title: Extensible module system
features.description: A themeable, modular architecture that schools and developers can extend.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
Full data ownership since schools self-host their own data
Highly configurable roles and permissions
Actively translated into 26 languages by a volunteer community
cons: Requires a server and technical setup to self-host
Smaller ecosystem than commercial school management vendors
Support relies on documentation and community rather than a dedicated vendor

faqs.question: Is Gibbon free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Gibbon is completely free and open source; schools can self-host it at no licensing cost.

faqs.question: Who built Gibbon?
faqs.answer: Gibbon was founded in 2010 by Ross Parker at International College Hong Kong and is now maintained by the Gibbon Foundation with community contributors.

faqs.question: Is Gibbon available in languages other than English?
faqs.answer: Yes, Gibbon is available in 26 actively translated languages, including right-to-left languages.

faqs.question: Do I need technical skills to run Gibbon?
faqs.answer: Yes, since it's self-hosted, schools need to install it on their own server (or via a hosting provider) and maintain their own backups.

use_cases.title: Independent and international schools
use_cases.description: Host their own student data instead of relying on an external SaaS vendor.

use_cases.title: Homeschool co-ops and small schools
use_cases.description: Use a free alternative to paid school management software.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Gibbon is a free, open-source, self-hosted school management platform for students, teachers, and parents, founded in 2010.

enrichment.short_description: A free, open-source student planner that combines class schedules, assignments, and grade tracking in one calendar.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Helium is a student planning app that brings classes, assignments, personal events, grades, and notes together in one color-coded calendar. It's funded entirely through Patreon supporters, which keeps it free and ad-free rather than run as a paid product.

The Django backend and related tools are open source on GitHub under the HeliumEdu organization, led by Alex Laird with community contributors welcome. Users retain full control of their data, including complete export and import in JSON format.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Helium is aimed at high school, college, and university students who want a single tool for tracking classes, assignments, deadlines, and grades instead of juggling separate calendar and spreadsheet apps.

features.title: Unified academic calendar
features.description: Month, week, day, agenda, and to-do views combine classes, assignments, and personal events.

features.title: Grade tracker and calculator
features.description: Real-time grade calculations, including 'what could I get' and 'what do I need' projections.

features.title: At-risk class alerts
features.description: Surfaces struggling classes automatically based on customizable thresholds.

features.title: Rich notebook
features.description: Formatted note-taking tied to specific classes and assignments.

features.title: Reminders
features.description: Push notifications and email alerts before deadlines.

features.title: External calendar sync
features.description: Supports Google, Apple, and iCal calendar subscriptions.

features.title: Native mobile apps
features.description: iOS and Android apps are available alongside the web app.

pros: Completely free and ad-free, funded by Patreon supporters
Open-source codebase available on GitHub
Native iOS and Android apps in addition to the web app
Grade projection tools help students track academic standing
cons: Aimed at individual students rather than institutions
Sustained by voluntary funding rather than a dedicated support team
Self-hosting the open-source backend requires technical setup

faqs.question: Is Helium free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Helium is completely free and ad-free, funded through Patreon supporters.

faqs.question: Is Helium open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, its Django backend and related tools are open source on GitHub under the HeliumEdu organization.

faqs.question: What platforms does Helium support?
faqs.answer: Helium is available as a web app plus native iOS and Android apps.

faqs.question: Can I export my data from Helium?
faqs.answer: Yes, Helium supports full data export and import in JSON format.

use_cases.title: College coursework tracking
use_cases.description: Track assignments, grades, and deadlines across multiple classes.

use_cases.title: High school planning
use_cases.description: Plan coursework and monitor at-risk classes before grades slip.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Helium is a free, open-source student planner app for tracking classes, assignments, and grades on web, iOS, and Android.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source learning management system, in use since 1998, that universities, schools, and enterprises can self-host.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ILIAS began as a project at the University of Cologne in 1998 and has grown into a long-running, GPL-licensed open-source learning management system used internationally.

Organizations can download ILIAS and self-host it on their own servers, or use one of a network of professional service providers that offer managed ILIAS hosting, customization, and training for those who prefer not to run it themselves.

The platform serves four main sectors: universities, enterprises, schools, and public administration, each of which can implement individual learning scenarios and training programs on top of the same core software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: ILIAS is used by universities and vocational schools running e-learning at scale, enterprises needing self-hosted corporate training, and public-sector organizations with data-sovereignty requirements.

features.title: Course and learning-module management
features.description: Tools for building and organizing online learning content.

features.title: Assessment and test engine
features.description: Built-in tools for creating and grading online tests.

features.title: Standards-compliant content support
features.description: Designed to fulfill common e-learning content and interoperability requirements.

features.title: Role-based user and group management
features.description: Configurable access for different learner and administrator roles.

features.title: Free GPL self-hosting
features.description: Downloadable and self-hostable at no licensing cost.

features.title: Professional partner network
features.description: Third-party providers offer managed hosting, customization, and training.

pros: Mature, long-track-record open-source LMS in use since 1998
Free to self-host under the GPL license
Supported by a professional partner network for hosting, customization, and training
Serves diverse sectors: education, enterprise, and government
cons: Site and documentation are primarily in German and English
Self-hosting requires server administration skills
Enterprise-grade support requires going through third-party partners rather than ILIAS itself

faqs.question: Is ILIAS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, ILIAS is free open-source software released under the GPL; anyone can download and self-host it.

faqs.question: How old is ILIAS?
faqs.answer: ILIAS began as a project at the University of Cologne in 1998, making it one of the longer-running open-source LMS projects.

faqs.question: Can I use ILIAS without running my own server?
faqs.answer: Yes, a network of professional service providers offers managed ILIAS hosting for organizations that don't want to self-host.

faqs.question: Who uses ILIAS?
faqs.answer: Universities, schools, enterprises, and public administration organizations use ILIAS for e-learning and training.

use_cases.title: University-scale e-learning
use_cases.description: Deliver large-scale online courses and assessments.

use_cases.title: Corporate training
use_cases.description: Run self-hosted training programs with full data control.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ILIAS is a free, open-source learning management system in use since 1998, self-hostable by universities, schools, and enterprises.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source automated grading platform that gives students instant feedback on programming assignments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: INGInious originated at Université catholique de Louvain and has been in active development since 2014, with partial funding from Wallonie. It's released under the AGPL license and describes itself as an online teaching assistant.

The platform automatically evaluates programming submissions and returns non-binary feedback intended to help students improve, rather than a simple pass/fail result. It supports learners from beginners through university-level coursework in a single environment.
Teachers can download INGInious from GitHub and self-host it, and it integrates with existing learning management systems including Moodle and edX/Open edX.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: INGInious is built for computer science instructors who need automated grading for programming exercises, and for students who want instant feedback while learning to code.

features.title: Automated grading and feedback
features.description: Automatically evaluates programming submissions and returns feedback rather than a simple pass/fail.

features.title: Non-binary feedback
features.description: Gives partial-credit style feedback designed to help students improve.

features.title: LMS integration
features.description: Integrates with Moodle and edX/Open edX.

features.title: Self-hostable deployment
features.description: Deployable by teachers from the open-source GitHub repository.

features.title: Multiple skill levels supported
features.description: Works for beginners through university-level programming coursework.

features.title: AGPL open-source licensing
features.description: Free to use and modify under the AGPL license.

pros: Free, self-hosted automated grading reduces manual instructor workload
Integrates with existing LMS platforms like Moodle and edX
Supports a wide range of programming skill levels
Actively developed since 2014 with institutional backing
cons: Focused on programming/CS education rather than general LMS features
Requires technical setup to self-host
Smaller documentation and community compared to mainstream LMS projects

faqs.question: Is INGInious free?
faqs.answer: Yes, INGInious is free and open source under the AGPL license.

faqs.question: What is INGInious used for?
faqs.answer: It automatically grades and gives feedback on programming assignments, functioning as an online teaching assistant.

faqs.question: Can INGInious integrate with other LMS tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with Moodle and edX/Open edX.

faqs.question: Who created INGInious?
faqs.answer: INGInious originated at Université catholique de Louvain and has been under active development since 2014.

use_cases.title: Automated coding assignment grading
use_cases.description: Automate grading of programming assignments for a course.

use_cases.title: Self-directed programming practice
use_cases.description: Practice coding and get instant feedback outside a formal class.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: INGInious is a free, open-source platform that automatically grades programming assignments and integrates with Moodle and edX.

enrichment.short_description: A free, open-source learning management system from Greece's GUnet, self-hostable for course delivery and e-learning.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Open eClass (formerly GUnet eClass) has been developed and distributed since 2003 by the GUnet eLearning group, part of the Greek Universities Network based in Athens, and is released as open-source software under the GNU GPL license.
The platform provides course creation, automated exercises, learning pathways, performance analytics, multimedia and e-book integration, and both synchronous and asynchronous communication tools.
Organizations can download and self-host Open eClass, or use GUnet's demo and free hosted instances instead of running their own installation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Open eClass is used by universities, adult-education centers, vocational training institutes, and enterprises — particularly within Greece's academic network — that need a free LMS.

features.title: Course creation and content management
features.description: Tools to build and organize course content.

features.title: Automated exercises and quizzes
features.description: Built-in tools for creating graded exercises.

features.title: Learning pathways and analytics
features.description: Tracks learner progress and performance.

features.title: Multimedia and e-book integration
features.description: Supports rich media content within courses.

features.title: Communication tools
features.description: Synchronous and asynchronous communication for instructors and students.

features.title: Self-hosting or free hosted instance
features.description: Downloadable self-hosted install or GUnet's free hosted option.

pros: Free and open source with a long operating track record since 2003
Available in multiple languages
Option to self-host or use GUnet's free managed instance
Backed by an academic network with institutional support
cons: Primary community and documentation emphasis is on the Greek academic sector
Self-hosting requires server setup and maintenance
Smaller international adoption compared to platforms like Moodle

faqs.question: Is Open eClass free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Open eClass is free, open-source software distributed under the GNU GPL license.

faqs.question: Who develops Open eClass?
faqs.answer: It's developed by GUnet, the Greek Universities Network eLearning group, based in Athens.

faqs.question: Can I try Open eClass without installing it?
faqs.answer: Yes, GUnet provides a demo instance and a free hosted instance in addition to the downloadable self-hosted version.

faqs.question: How long has Open eClass been around?
faqs.answer: It has been developed and distributed since 2003.

use_cases.title: Academic course delivery
use_cases.description: Deliver accredited online courses at universities.

use_cases.title: Vocational and enterprise training
use_cases.description: Manage employee or student training programs.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Open eClass is a free, open-source LMS developed by Greece's GUnet since 2003, available to self-host or use via a free hosted instance.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source learning management system for testing, courses, and collaboration, self-hostable or available via managed hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenOLAT was forked in 2011 from the original OLAT platform, which the University of Zurich launched in 1999. The fork is developed by frentix GmbH, a Zurich-based spin-off founded in 2006 by one of OLAT's original creators.
OpenOLAT is open-source software available on GitHub for self-hosting. frentix also offers commercial managed hosting for organizations that would rather not run and maintain the platform themselves.
The platform includes testing and assessment tools, video integration, webconferencing via BigBlueButton, and enterprise authentication support (LDAP, Active Directory, OAuth2, and Shibboleth).

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OpenOLAT serves schools, universities, corporate training departments, and government or administrative bodies, with a market focus concentrated in German-speaking regions.

features.title: eTesting and assessment
features.description: Built-in tools for creating and grading tests and exams.

features.title: Video integration
features.description: Video content with interactive elements embedded in courses.

features.title: Webconferencing via BigBlueButton
features.description: Live sessions integrated directly into the platform.

features.title: Enterprise authentication
features.description: Supports LDAP, Active Directory, OAuth2, and Shibboleth for user management.

features.title: Calendar and attendance tracking
features.description: Scheduling and attendance tools built into the platform.

features.title: Document collaboration
features.description: Document management and collaborative editing tools.

features.title: Free self-hosting or managed hosting
features.description: Run it yourself for free, or use frentix's commercial hosting service.

pros: Mature codebase with roots going back to 1999
Free to self-host with no licensing cost
Built-in enterprise authentication integrations (LDAP/AD/OAuth2/Shibboleth)
Commercial managed hosting available for organizations that don't want to self-host
cons: Primary documentation and market focus is German-speaking regions
Self-hosting requires Java application server expertise
Managed hosting pricing isn't published and requires contacting frentix directly

faqs.question: Is OpenOLAT free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted OpenOLAT software is free and open source; frentix separately offers paid managed hosting for those who don't want to self-host.

faqs.question: Who maintains OpenOLAT?
faqs.answer: frentix GmbH, a Zurich-based spin-off of the University of Zurich, has developed OpenOLAT since forking it from OLAT in 2011.

faqs.question: What authentication systems does OpenOLAT support?
faqs.answer: It supports LDAP, Active Directory, OAuth2, and Shibboleth for user management.

faqs.question: Does OpenOLAT include video conferencing?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates BigBlueButton for webconferencing.

use_cases.title: University and school course delivery
use_cases.description: Deliver testing, courses, and grading online.

use_cases.title: Corporate and government training
use_cases.description: Run internal training with enterprise authentication support.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenOLAT is an open-source LMS forked from OLAT in 2011, free to self-host, with optional commercial hosting from frentix in Zurich.

enrichment.short_description: A free, open-source, self-hosted assessment platform for building quizzes, exams, and surveys with automatic grading.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: QST is a self-hosted, open-source assessment platform released under GPLv2, built specifically for quizzes, exams, surveys, and assignments rather than being a full learning management system.

It supports multi-tenancy, so a single server installation can host unlimited independent organizations, and it includes a gradebook with automatic marking for objective questions alongside manual grading for essays.
The license allows running QST as a paid service without releasing source code, and permits rebranding or commercial use without additional licensing fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: QST is built for teachers, trainers, and organizations that need a dedicated testing and quiz tool — including STEM educators who need math and chemistry notation — rather than a complete course-management system.

features.title: Multi-tenant architecture
features.description: One server installation can host unlimited independent organizations.

features.title: Integrated gradebook
features.description: Automatic marking for objective questions and manual grading for essays.

features.title: Multiple question types
features.description: Multiple choice, matching, true/false, short answer, essay, and surveys with skip logic.

features.title: Built-in math and chemistry editor
features.description: WYSIWYG equation editor powered by MathJax for STEM content.

features.title: Memory recall mode
features.description: A question mode that displays content once and then removes it, testing active recall.

features.title: Broad import/export formats
features.description: Supports QST XML, Moodle XML, QTI, Word XML, and PDF.

pros: Free with no licensing restrictions, including commercial or rebranded use
Strong STEM support via a built-in math and chemistry equation editor
Flexible question-import formats, including Moodle XML and QTI
Multi-tenant design suits hosting providers or districts running many schools
cons: Focused narrowly on assessments rather than full course delivery
Smaller community than mainstream quiz tools
Requires self-hosting and server maintenance

faqs.question: Is QST free?
faqs.answer: Yes, QST is free and open source under GPLv2, with no licensing fees even for commercial or rebranded use.

faqs.question: Is QST a full LMS?
faqs.answer: No, it's a dedicated assessment platform for quizzes, exams, surveys, and assignments rather than a complete course-management system.

faqs.question: Can QST import questions from other formats?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports QST XML, Moodle XML, QTI, Word XML, and PDF import/export.

faqs.question: Does QST support math and science notation?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a built-in WYSIWYG math/chemistry equation editor powered by MathJax.

use_cases.title: District-wide testing server
use_cases.description: Run a shared, multi-tenant testing server for multiple schools.

use_cases.title: STEM assessment authoring
use_cases.description: Build quizzes and exams with equations and automatic grading.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: QST is a free, open-source, self-hosted assessment platform for quizzes, exams, and surveys with automatic grading and STEM support.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source, Django-based courseware platform with Git-backed content, automatic grading, and code sandboxing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RELATE is an open-source courseware platform built on Django by Andreas Kloeckner and hosted on GitHub. Courses are authored in plain-text YAML and Markdown, with content changes tracked deeply through Git so instructors can preview new material while students continue on a stable version.

The platform includes sandboxed code execution with automatic grading and plotting support, interactive quizzes and surveys, in-class instant messaging via XMPP, and engagement analytics. A single installation can host multiple courses at once.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: RELATE suits instructors who are comfortable with Git and text-based authoring and who want fine-grained control over grading rules, sandboxed code execution, and multi-course hosting on one installation.

features.title: Git-integrated content
features.description: Course content changes are tracked with Git, with staged previews before students see them.

features.title: Text-based authoring
features.description: Courses are written in YAML and Markdown rather than a WYSIWYG editor.

features.title: Sandboxed code execution
features.description: Automatic grading and plotting support for programming assignments run in a sandbox.

features.title: Quizzes and surveys
features.description: Interactive assessments alongside live in-class activities.

features.title: Engagement analytics
features.description: Tracks student participation and performance.

features.title: In-class messaging
features.description: Instant messaging support via XMPP for live classroom activities.

features.title: Multi-course hosting
features.description: One RELATE installation can host multiple courses.

pros: Fine-grained, text-based control over grading and access rules
Deep Git integration supports safe, staged content iteration
Sandboxed code execution suits programming and data-science courses
Free and self-hostable open-source software
cons: Workflow assumes familiarity with Git and YAML/Markdown authoring
Smaller community and ecosystem than mainstream LMS projects
Requires self-hosting and Django deployment expertise

faqs.question: Is RELATE free?
faqs.answer: Yes, RELATE is free, open-source software that organizations can self-host.

faqs.question: Who built RELATE?
faqs.answer: RELATE was developed by Andreas Kloeckner and is maintained on GitHub as an open-source project.

faqs.question: How is course content managed in RELATE?
faqs.answer: Content is authored in YAML and Markdown and tracked with Git, letting instructors preview new material while students use a stable version.

faqs.question: Does RELATE support automatic grading of code?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes sandboxed code execution with automatic grading and plotting support.

use_cases.title: Programming and data-science courses
use_cases.description: Automatically grade code submissions in a sandboxed environment.

use_cases.title: Version-controlled course management
use_cases.description: Manage multiple courses with Git-tracked content.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: RELATE is a free, open-source, Django-based courseware platform with Git-tracked content and sandboxed automatic code grading.

enrichment.short_description: A free, open-source student information system for K-12 schools to manage grades, scheduling, attendance, and billing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RosarioSIS began around 2012 as a project for a school in Colombia, originating as a fork of Centre/SIS, and has since grown into a free, open-source student information system built with PHP and either PostgreSQL or MySQL.

The software is distributed under the GNU GPL v2 license and covers students, grades, scheduling, attendance, billing, discipline, and food service, alongside a responsive design, statistical charts, PDF generation, online registration, and Moodle LMS integration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: RosarioSIS is primarily designed for K-12 schools that want a self-hosted, free alternative to commercial student information systems, though its flexible architecture can be adapted for universities and academies as well.

features.title: Student records and grading
features.description: Core student information and gradebook management.

features.title: Scheduling and attendance
features.description: Tools for building class schedules and tracking attendance.

features.title: Billing and food service
features.description: Modules for school billing and food service management.

features.title: Discipline tracking
features.description: Records and tracks student discipline incidents.

features.title: Responsive interface
features.description: Mobile-friendly design with statistical charts and PDF generation.

features.title: Online registration
features.description: Lets families register students online.

features.title: Moodle integration
features.description: Connects with the Moodle LMS.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPL v2
Broad module coverage beyond just gradebook: attendance, billing, food service, discipline
Translated into a dozen languages for international schools
Responsive design works on mobile devices
cons: Primarily designed for K-12 workflows, so higher-ed use requires more customization
Self-hosting requires a PHP and PostgreSQL/MySQL server plus ongoing maintenance
Some add-ons are offered as paid extras alongside the free core

faqs.question: Is RosarioSIS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core RosarioSIS platform is free and open source under the GNU GPL v2 license.

faqs.question: What database does RosarioSIS use?
faqs.answer: It runs on PHP with either a PostgreSQL or MySQL database.

faqs.question: What languages is RosarioSIS available in?
faqs.answer: It's translated into English plus French, Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, Russian, Ukrainian, Czech, Greek, Turkish, German, Vietnamese, and Bulgarian.

faqs.question: Does RosarioSIS integrate with an LMS?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with Moodle.

use_cases.title: K-12 student information system
use_cases.description: Self-host a full student information system covering grades, attendance, and billing.

use_cases.title: Multilingual school communities
use_cases.description: Use built-in translations for non-English-speaking school communities.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: RosarioSIS is a free, open-source student information system for K-12 schools, covering grades, scheduling, attendance, and billing.
